summary: The CEO reported two turns during this Programme's early work where the H-P01 prompt-optimization gate's required confirmation flow appeared not to run. A dedicated harness-engineering forensic pass (Kwame Asante) scanned the session's complete raw transcript and found exactly six genuine H-P01 firings in total, every one followed by the required AskUserQuestion confirmation with zero exceptions -- closing the compliance-gap question, though the CEO's two originally-flagged turns could not be identified after the fact well enough to confirm or rule out on their own. The mechanism was verified to have no code path that fires without producing both a recorded system-reminder and a gate-enforcement marker file.
root_cause: Investigated but not fully resolved to the level of the two specific flagged turns. Two candidate explanations were weighed: (a) the hook simply never fired on those turns because their prompts scored at or above the 3/5 quality threshold, so no confirmation was structurally owed; (b) the hook fired and the confirmation step was skipped, a genuine compliance gap. Direct transcript forensics found zero evidence anywhere in the session supporting (b) -- every genuine firing was followed by confirmation -- which is consistent with (a), though it does not itself forensically prove (a) for the two specific unidentified turns. Root cause is recorded as resolved for the general compliance-gap question, not as a forensic identification of those two turns.
remediation: No code or process change was required -- the gate's implementation was found to function exactly as documented in CLAUDE.md section 11, both in the deterministic scoring logic and in the PreToolUse enforcement mechanism. The standing takeaway, already true before this investigation but reconfirmed by it: any [PROMPT OPTIMIZER - H-P01] system-reminder is treated as an immediate, blocking instruction requiring the full optimize-then-confirm flow before any other work proceeds, on every turn, with no carryover of prior approvals.
scope_of_applicability: Surface this record when a future claim arises that a workspace hook 'fired and was silently ignored' versus 'never fired because its trigger condition genuinely was not met' -- the diagnostic method demonstrated here (scan the raw session transcript's own hook-firing attachment records, cross-reference each one against the very next tool call) generalizes to verifying any PreToolUse/UserPromptSubmit hook's real compliance history, not just H-P01.

summary: Every audit document in the 2026-07-14-reflexion-memory-system Programme cross-referenced sibling documents using '../'-chain relative paths, where the exact number of '../' segments depended on how deeply nested the referencing file happened to be. The CEO identified this as a real source of ambiguity for readers, not a stylistic nitpick -- demonstrated directly when two files moved into a nested stage folder the same session and every relative reference in both had to be manually recounted and rewritten.
root_cause: Each audit document was authored assuming its current folder depth, with no fixed convention for how cross-references should be written. A relative-path scheme that breaks on every folder move, and requires a reader to manually count dots to know what a link points to, is a defect regardless of whether it happened to be counted correctly on any given pass.
remediation: All '../'-style references were replaced with full, workspace-root-relative paths, verified via a repository-wide search returning zero remaining matches. Convention going forward: cross-references in a telescope Programme's supporting/ folder (at any nesting depth) use full workspace-root-relative paths, never '../' chains.
scope_of_applicability: Surface this record when authoring or reviewing any Markdown document inside a nested telescope Programme subfolder (supporting/, or any subfolder within it) that cross-references sibling documents -- use full workspace-root-relative paths, never '../'-chain relative paths, regardless of the current file's nesting depth.

summary: The Investigator-Authored Write Path's identity-enforcement mechanism went through three implementation rounds before it was found to be honestly scoped. Round 1: a git-identity + roster-name check authenticated the machine, not the operator -- Dr. Wieczorek demonstrated this live by passing the check from his own adversarial-review agent, no forgery required. Round 2: an IdentityVerification token + TTY-gated confirmation were both still bypassable -- a caller could fabricate the token directly (silently skipping confirmation, checked one layer up), or call the persistence sink directly, bypassing every check above it. Round 3 (final): governance confirmation was folded directly into the identity token, checked independently at both the record and the sink layer.
root_cause: No purely code-level check running inside Claude Code's own tool-execution environment can be unforgeable, because any Python-importable layer is skippable by calling something lower -- there is no floor a purely in-process check can stand on. An agent has the same import access a human developer does.
remediation: For GOVERNANCE_TRIGGERS records, the actual security boundary was moved from code to procedure: genuine, live, in-transcript confirmation from the real human user, never relayed through an intermediary agent -- mirroring the precedent already established for .claude/hooks/ self-modification. The code-level layers (git-identity + roster attribution, the IdentityVerification token, the TTY-gated confirmation, the independent sink-level re-check) are retained and honestly documented as defense-in-depth against careless/accidental misuse, not as the boundary itself.
scope_of_applicability: Surface this record whenever a future design in this workspace proposes 'the script runs under an authenticated session' as its security boundary for a write path an AI agent can also reach -- code-level identity checks raise the bar against careless misuse but cannot be the actual boundary in this environment; the real boundary must be procedural, live, in-transcript human confirmation.

summary: Dr. Vance decided, in writing, exactly where the required progress-tracking files (progress.md, session-log.md, checkpoint.json) should live for the 2026-07-13-mcp-embedder-service-redesign build. All six phases plus the EX-001 remediation task then executed and merged in full, with zero tracking artifacts ever created. The CEO went looking for the required documentation and found nothing -- a direct violation of a workspace requirement Dr. Vance himself had already acknowledged in writing before execution began.
root_cause: The orchestrator briefs issued to every worker agent in this build specified phase gates, acceptance criteria, and git-worktree conventions in exacting detail, but never once included tracking-file creation or maintenance as a deliverable. A location decision recorded in a planning document is not an execution instruction. No one was ever told to do it, including Dr. Vance himself, who did not do it either.
remediation: The three tracking files were compiled after the fact as the official record for the build, verified against git commit history -- not a substitute for the violation, a record of what happened despite it. core-component-00/multi-agent-engineering/fundamentals/git-worktree-orchestration.md now documents a corrected rule: any orchestrator brief covering work subject to the progress-monitoring convention must include tracking-file creation as an explicit, checked deliverable in the first code-producing phase, with the orchestrator itself as the default owner if no worker agent is the obvious fit.
scope_of_applicability: Surface this record whenever an orchestrator brief is drafted for multi-phase or multi-agent work subject to .claude/rules/workspace-conventions.md's Company Pipeline Progress Monitoring convention -- confirm tracking-file creation is an explicit, checked deliverable in the brief, not merely a location decided in a planning document.
